hero_summary: Maqam Iraq belongs to the Sikah family, but it is not Sikah itself. Its tonic is Iraq (B half-flat), and its identity emerges from the position of the tonic, the lower structure, and the sayr.
short_identity: A maqam of the Sikah family whose tonic is Iraq (B half-flat), built in the documented description from Jins Sikah on Iraq followed by Jins Bayati on Dukah (D).

quick_facts.character: Sikah-colored, but with an independent identity confirmed by the tonic, sayr, and cadences
quick_facts.common_start: It begins from Iraq (B half-flat), then unfolds toward Dukah (D) and above before returning to the tonic

sayr.summary: Maqam Iraq belongs to the Sikah family, but it must not be confused with Sikah itself. Its identity rests on the fact that the tonic is not Sikah (E half-flat), but Iraq (B half-flat). For that reason it retains the general Sikah color while differing clearly in the position of the tonic, the lower structure, and the sayr. In the documented description it is built from Jins Sikah on Iraq (B half-flat), followed by Jins Bayati on Dukah (D). Its sayr may be ascending or ascending-descending: it begins from the tonic, unfolds toward Dukah (D) and above, then returns to establish Iraq as the final center of attraction.
resting_tones: Iraq (B half-flat): the tonic and final center of attraction of the maqam
Dukah (D): an important secondary point of rest, because it is the place of the Bayati jins in the documented structure
Its auditory color remains related to the Sikah world, but the final point of repose is established on Iraq rather than on Sikah
motion_notes: The difference from Sikah is not cosmetic, but structural, because the center of gravity moves from Sikah to Iraq
Iraq is not understood well from listing the scale alone, but from the way the upper region is opened and the way the return to the tonic is handled convincingly
Any unstable intonation of Iraq (B half-flat) may pull the ear toward nearby Sikah-family maqamat
The maqam’s identity appears more in phrase, sayr, and cadence than in a classroom theoretical diagram alone
common_path: Establish Iraq (B half-flat) as the tonic of the maqam
Show the Sikah jins on the tonic within the lower structure
Expand toward Dukah (D) through Jins Bayati
Complete the path in an ascending or ascending-descending treatment as needed
Return to the tonic while confirming that Iraq is the natural final resting point of the maqam
common_modulations: The documented description agrees that Iraq belongs to the Sikah family and that its tonic is Iraq (B half-flat)
It differs from Sikah in the position of the tonic and the lower structure, not merely in family belonging

It differs from Huzam, despite family proximity, because Huzam usually centers on Sikah (E half-flat) and is commonly explained through Hijaz on Nawa, whereas Iraq has a different tonic and a different documented structure
Some studies note that Iraq shares its scale with Awj, while the real modal difference remains in sayr, points of emphasis, and cadence

The material verified here shows no sharp disagreement over its Iraq identity or its belonging to the Sikah family, though there are differences of presentation between Arabic and Turkish schools, and its contemporary presence varies regionally

examples.title: Qul lilladhi bil-bu'd wal-hijr jazana
examples.note: The third Btayhi from Nawbat al-Iraq, cited as a piece corresponding to Eastern Maqam Iraq

examples.note: The fourth Btayhi from Nawbat al-Iraq, presented in the reference text as centered on Iraq in a way matching Eastern Maqam Iraq
